entries.title: COMMENTARY ON PAUL'S EPISTLES on second-corinthians 8:12
entries.excerpt: Paul is exhorting the Corinthians to give what they are able but not to overdo it, because he did not want them to feel that they were acting under compulsion and thus come to be resentful.

entries.title: The Stromata Book 2 on second-corinthians 8:12
entries.excerpt:
"But if there be present readiness of mind, according to what a man hath it is acceptable, and not according to what he hath not: for it is not that there be ease to others, but tribulation to you, but of equality at the present time "and so forth.
Those, who have entertained the wish whose purpose is equal, share in the same honour with those who have the ability, although others have the advantage in point of resources.

entries.title: Treatise XII Three Books of Testimonies Against the Jews on second-corinthians 8:12
entries.excerpt: In the second Epistle of Paul to the Corinthians: "If there be a ready will, it is acceptable according to what a man hath, not according to that which he hath not; nor let there be to others a mitigation, but to you a burdening.

entries.title: Homily 17 on 2 Corinthians on second-corinthians 8:12
entries.excerpt:
See wisdom unspeakable. In that (having pointed out those who were doing beyond their power, I mean the Thessalonians, and having praised them for this and said, "I bear them record that even beyond their power;") he exhorteth the Corinthians to do only "after" their power, leaving the example to do its own work; for he knew that not so much exhortation, as emulation, inciteth unto imitation of the like; wherefore he saith, "For if the readiness is there, it is acceptable according as a man hath, not according as he hath not."

'Fear not,' he means, 'because I have said these things, for what I said was an encomium upon their munificence, but God requires things after a man's power,' "according as he hath, not according as he hath not." For the word "is acceptable," here implies 'is required.' And he softens it greatly, in confident reliance upon this example, and as winning them more surely by leaving them at liberty.

entries.title: Commentary on 2 Corinthians on second-corinthians 8:12
entries.excerpt: Notice the wisdom: having praised the Macedonians—I mean the Thessalonians—for giving "beyond their means," from these he demands nothing beyond their means. If, he says, you have eagerness, give according to your means; God accepts this.

entries.title: Commentary on 2 Corinthians on second-corinthians 8:12
entries.excerpt: Then he explains what he means by "out of what you have," saying, "For if the readiness is there, it is acceptable according to what a man has." As if to say: I say that you should be prompt to give, but I do not wish to burden you in this matter, namely, that you would give beyond your means, because perhaps your will inclines you to this; but in this matter the work cannot follow the will. Therefore he says, "out of what you have." For, i.e., because, if the readiness is there, it is acceptable according to what a man has, the reason being that the will is accepted in the accomplishment of the work; but the work is not perfected except from what a man has; hence he says, it is acceptable according to what a man has: "If you have much, give generously; if you have a little, then try to impart a little gladly" (Tob. 4:9).
